front: Opera: ends at a bullfight where Don José stabs the title character to death when she throws a ring back at him in the duet 'C'est toi? C'est moi!'
Opera: based on a Prosper Mérimée novella; set in Seville but sung in French
Opera: Rodion Shchedrin arranged its music into a one-act ballet scored for string orchestra and percussion
Opera: Oscar Hammerstein's all-Black adaptation, filmed in 1954 with Harry Belafonte and Dorothy Dandridge, gives the title character the surname 'Jones'
Opera: Pablo de Sarasate composed a violin Fantasy on its themes, opening with the Aragonaise; Vladimir Horowitz wrote virtuosic piano variations on its Danse Bohème
Opera: Johannes Brahms saw it over twenty times after its Vienna premiere
Opera: legend holds that Célestine Galli-Marié, the creator of its title role, fainted during a performance on the night the composer died
Opera: Susan McClary wrote her only single-work monograph about it
Opera: mentor Charles Gounod said after the premiere that its composer deserved credit only for 'the sauce that masks the fish'
Opera: fate motif in the prelude — long D, C-sharp, B-flat, C-sharp, A — over tremolo D minor triads, followed by two soft timpani strokes
Opera: the Habanera ('L'amour est un oiseau rebelle') opens with a descending chromatic scale from D and compares love to a 'rebellious bird that has never known the law'
work: The Habanera ('L'amour est un oiseau rebelle')

front: Opera: the Habanera was inspired by a Sebastián Iradier song that its composer mistakenly believed was a folk melody
work: The Habanera ('L'amour est un oiseau rebelle')

front: Opera: Saint-Saëns derived the opening violin motif of his Havanaise from this opera's Habanera
work: The Habanera ('L'amour est un oiseau rebelle')

front: Opera: the Toreador Song ('Votre toast, je peux vous le rendre') is sung by Escamillo in Act II and reprised as the title character is murdered outside the bullring
work: The Toreador Song ('Votre toast, je peux vous le rendre')

front: Opera: the Flower Song — Don José declares he kept a 'withered and dry' flower Carmen tossed him while imprisoned
work: The Flower Song ('La fleur que tu m'avais jetée')

front: Opera: Don José defies his superior Zuniga by freeing Carmen's hands after she sings him the Seguidilla
work: Don José and the Seguidilla Scene

front: Opera: in Act III, Carmen draws a fortune-telling card predicting her own death in the aria 'En vain pour éviter'
Movement: begins with arpeggiated harp under a solo flute melody, restated in clarinet then English horn; bridges Acts II and III of a Bizet opera
